title: Sample Claude Exam - Form B
description: Generated 60-question variation bank aligned to the Claude architecture foundations study guide.

source.notes: Questions were generated by domain-focused workers, then validated and enriched against the structured study guide.

domains.label: Agentic Architecture & Orchestration
domains.description: Design model-driven agent loops, coordinator-subagent systems, handoffs, hooks, decomposition, and session state.

domains.label: Tool Design & MCP Integration
domains.description: Design reliable tools, MCP integrations, structured errors, scoped tool access, and built-in tool usage.

domains.label: Claude Code Configuration & Workflows
domains.description: Configure Claude Code memory, rules, skills, commands, plan mode, iterative workflows, and CI behavior.

domains.label: Prompt Engineering & Structured Output
domains.description: Improve precision, use few-shot prompting, enforce schema output, validate retries, batch work, and design review architectures.

domains.label: Context Management & Reliability
domains.description: Preserve facts, manage long context, escalate appropriately, propagate errors, explore codebases, calibrate review, and keep provenance.

skills: Continue on tool_use and terminate on end_turn.
Append tool results between iterations.
Avoid natural-language completion heuristics or arbitrary caps as the main stop mechanism.
knowledge: Agent loop lifecycle from Claude request to tool execution to returned tool result.
How tool results become part of conversation history for the next reasoning step.
Difference between model-driven tool decisions and preconfigured decision trees.
guideReferences: Domain 1, Task 1.1: Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution
questions.guideSummary: Agentic loops should continue when Claude returns tool_use, append tool results, and stop when the model returns end_turn rather than relying on natural-language phrases.
questions.prompt: A procurement assistant reviews vendor contracts. In testing, it sometimes stops after saying, "I should check the supplier registry," even though the model response includes a tool call for `lookup_supplier`. The loop controller currently ends the run whenever the text appears to contain a conclusion. What change best aligns the controller with an agentic loop?

choices.text: Add a prompt instruction requiring the model to write `FINAL` only when it has no more tools to call.
choices.explanation: A completion keyword is still a natural-language heuristic. It can reduce errors but does not provide the loop control signal the guide recommends.

choices.text: Set a maximum of one tool call for contract reviews so the loop cannot continue after the first lookup.
choices.explanation: An arbitrary cap can prevent runaway execution, but it is not the main completion mechanism and would break valid multi-step reviews.

choices.text: Precompute a decision tree that maps contract fields to the exact sequence of registry tools.
choices.explanation: That replaces model-driven tool selection with brittle orchestration and does not address the incorrect stop condition.

choices.text: Continue the loop whenever the response stop_reason is `tool_use`, execute the requested tool, append the tool result, and stop on `end_turn`.
choices.explanation: Correct. The loop should be driven by stop_reason and tool results, not by interpreting natural-language text as completion.

skills: Write differentiated tool descriptions.
Rename or split tools when capabilities overlap.
Review prompts for wording that undermines tool descriptions.
knowledge: Tool descriptions are a primary selection signal.
Inputs, examples, edge cases, and boundaries improve selection reliability.
Ambiguous or overlapping descriptions cause misrouting.
Prompt wording can bias tool selection.
guideReferences: Domain 2, Task 2.1: Design effective tool interfaces with clear descriptions and boundaries
questions.guideSummary: Tool names and descriptions should define clear boundaries so Claude can choose the right capability without guessing from overlapping contracts.
questions.prompt: A retail operations agent has three MCP tools: `lookup_order`, `inspect_order`, and `get_order_info`. Their descriptions all say some variation of "gets order details." In evaluation, Claude frequently calls `inspect_order` when it only needs customer-visible status, and calls `lookup_order` when it needs internal fraud flags that only `inspect_order` returns. What change best improves tool selection reliability?

choices.text: Keep the tools unchanged and add a system prompt rule that says to pick the most relevant order tool.
choices.explanation: A generic prompt rule does not resolve the ambiguity in the tool contracts Claude sees at selection time.

choices.text: Merge all three tools into one `order_tool` with a free-text `operation` argument.
choices.explanation: That hides the important boundary inside an argument and makes selection depend on prose again.

choices.text: Rename and describe the tools by purpose, inputs, outputs, and boundaries, such as `get_customer_order_status` versus `inspect_internal_order_risk`.
choices.explanation: Correct. Differentiated names and descriptions give Claude clear selection signals and reduce overlap between customer-status and internal-risk workflows.

choices.text: Remove the internal-risk tool so Claude cannot accidentally call it.
choices.explanation: This avoids one failure mode by removing a needed capability, rather than designing a clearer interface.

questions.guideSummary: Covers placing shared Claude Code instructions in project scope while keeping personal preferences user-scoped.
questions.prompt: A platform team discovers that Claude Code follows a deployment checklist for one senior engineer but not for the rest of the team. The checklist is currently in that engineer's user-level CLAUDE.md. The team wants every repo contributor to get the same deployment guidance, while preserving individual preferences such as preferred shell aliases. What is the best configuration change?

choices.text: Move the shared deployment checklist into the repository's project CLAUDE.md and leave personal preferences in user-level memory.
choices.explanation: Correct. Project memory is shared through the repo, while user-level memory is appropriate for personal settings that should not affect teammates.

choices.text: Ask each engineer to copy the senior engineer's user-level CLAUDE.md into their own home directory.
choices.explanation: Manual copying is brittle and mixes team standards with personal preferences. Updates will drift across machines.

choices.text: Put the checklist into a custom slash command and require engineers to run it before every deployment task.
choices.explanation: A command helps with on-demand workflows, but always-relevant deployment standards should be loaded as shared project memory.

choices.text: Tell each engineer to have Claude Code read the senior engineer's recent deployment commits and infer the checklist from how those deployments were done.
choices.explanation: Inferring a process from past commits is unreliable and reconstructs the standard differently for each person. An explicit shared checklist in project memory is the durable source teammates should load.

skills: Define report versus skip categories.
Disable noisy categories while improving prompts.
Use concrete examples for severity classification.
knowledge: Explicit criteria work better than vague conservatism.
False positives undermine developer trust.
Severity should be defined with concrete examples.
guideReferences: Domain 4, Task 4.1: Design prompts with explicit criteria to improve precision and reduce false positives
questions.guideSummary: Covers replacing vague review instructions with explicit report and skip criteria to reduce false positives.
questions.prompt: A CI review prompt says, 'Be conservative and flag anything that might be risky.' After rollout, developers ignore the bot because it comments on harmless TODOs, style preferences, and test helper patterns as if they were production defects. Which prompt change best improves precision without hiding real defects?

choices.text: Tell Claude to only report issues it is 100% certain about so developers see fewer comments.
choices.explanation: A confidence-only instruction is vague and can suppress real issues. The guide emphasizes explicit criteria over vague conservatism.

choices.text: Add explicit report categories, skip categories, and severity definitions with examples of what should and should not be reported.
choices.explanation: Correct. The failure is vague criteria producing false positives. Concrete report versus skip rules and severity examples calibrate the review.

choices.text: Lower the model temperature so the same comments appear consistently across reruns.
choices.explanation: Determinism does not define what counts as a reportable issue. It can make noisy behavior more consistent.

choices.text: Route all comments through a senior developer before posting them to pull requests.
choices.explanation: Human review can catch noise, but it does not fix the prompt calibration problem that creates the false positives.

skills: Maintain structured case facts.
Persist multi-issue state separately from summaries.
Trim tool results to relevant fields.
Place summaries early and use explicit section headers.
knowledge: Progressive summaries can blur amounts, dates, and commitments.
Lost-in-the-middle effects hide middle input details.
Verbose tool results can consume context without adding value.
Complete conversation history helps coherence.
guideReferences: Domain 5, Task 5.1: Manage conversation context to preserve critical information across long interactions
questions.guideSummary: Preserve exact amounts, dates, commitments, and issue status outside lossy long-conversation summaries.
questions.prompt: A loan-servicing assistant handles a 70-turn dispute involving a $148 escrow credit, a promised May 3 callback, and two unrelated address corrections. After several automatic summaries, the next response says only that a small credit is expected soon and asks the borrower to restate the address issue. What design change best prevents this class of reliability failure?

choices.text: Increase the summarization frequency so each summary is shorter and easier for the model to use.
choices.explanation: More frequent summaries can make facts blur faster if exact values and commitments are not preserved in a structured layer.

choices.text: Maintain a structured case-facts record with exact amounts, dates, commitments, issue owners, and open items, then pass a concise current summary early in context.
choices.explanation: Correct. Critical facts should survive outside progressive prose summaries, and the current summary should make the active state easy to find.

choices.text: Pass the full transcript and every tool result into each turn so no information is ever removed.
choices.explanation: Full history helps coherence, but verbose transcripts and tool results can overwhelm context and make important facts harder to attend to.

choices.text: Ask the borrower to confirm all prior facts whenever the thread exceeds a fixed number of turns.
choices.explanation: Repeatedly pushing state recovery onto the user is poor reliability design and still does not preserve commitments internally.

skills: Continue on tool_use and terminate on end_turn.
Append tool results between iterations.
Avoid natural-language completion heuristics or arbitrary caps as the main stop mechanism.
knowledge: Agent loop lifecycle from Claude request to tool execution to returned tool result.
How tool results become part of conversation history for the next reasoning step.
Difference between model-driven tool decisions and preconfigured decision trees.
guideReferences: Domain 1, Task 1.1: Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution
questions.guideSummary: Tool results must be appended to conversation history between iterations so the model can reason over fresh results instead of repeating calls.
questions.prompt: An inventory agent calls `check_stock` for a warehouse, receives the correct count from the tool runner, then immediately calls `check_stock` again with the same arguments. Logs show the application executed the tool but did not include the returned count in the next Claude request. What is the best fix?

choices.text: Tell the model that repeated calls to the same tool are discouraged unless the user asks for confirmation.
choices.explanation: Prompt guidance does not solve the missing state. The model is repeating the call because it has not seen the result.

choices.text: Cache all `check_stock` calls in the application layer and silently return the cached value without notifying the model.
choices.explanation: Caching may save backend work, but the model still lacks the returned count and cannot decide the next action reliably.

choices.text: Add the `check_stock` output as a tool result in the conversation before asking the model for the next step.
choices.explanation: Correct. The model can only reason from tool outputs that are appended back into the conversation history.

choices.text: Terminate the loop after any tool call that returns a successful HTTP status.
choices.explanation: A successful tool call is not the same as task completion. The model still needs to use the result to continue or finish.

questions.guideSummary: Tool descriptions must include boundaries and examples, and prompts should not bias Claude toward a weaker tool choice.
questions.prompt: A claims review agent has `summarize_claim_file` and `verify_policy_coverage`. The tool descriptions are clear, but the system prompt says, "When reviewing claims, start by summarizing the file." For coverage disputes, Claude summarizes first and then gives an answer without ever calling the verification tool. What is the best fix?

choices.text: Rename `summarize_claim_file` to `do_not_use_for_coverage` so Claude avoids it in disputes.
choices.explanation: A negative name is brittle and awkward. The prompt should stop undermining the existing boundary.

choices.text: Delete `summarize_claim_file` from the server because summarization is less important than verification.
choices.explanation: The summary tool is still useful for many reviews; the issue is the biased instruction, not the tool's existence.

choices.text: Revise the prompt so it no longer implies summary must always come first, and state that coverage decisions require `verify_policy_coverage`.
choices.explanation: Correct. Prompt wording can bias tool selection. Removing the blanket sequencing instruction lets the tool descriptions and required coverage boundary drive the call.

choices.text: Add a longer natural-language explanation to `verify_policy_coverage` but leave the prompt unchanged.
choices.explanation: A better description may help, but the prompt still tells Claude to start somewhere else for every claim.

questions.guideSummary: Covers using imports and focused rule files so Claude Code memory stays modular and inspectable.
questions.prompt: A monorepo's root CLAUDE.md has grown to 900 lines. It includes frontend style rules, database migration rules, release rules, and security review guidance. Engineers complain that Claude Code loads irrelevant instructions and occasionally follows the wrong standard. The rules are still valid, but they need better organization. What should the team do first?

choices.text: Split the guidance into focused files and import the relevant standards from CLAUDE.md or organize them under .claude/rules.
choices.explanation: Correct. The guide recommends modular memory with imports and rule files so large instruction sets remain focused and maintainable.

choices.text: Delete the specialized rules and replace them with a short instruction telling Claude Code to infer conventions from nearby files.
choices.explanation: Nearby files can help, but deleting explicit standards removes useful team guidance and makes behavior less predictable.

choices.text: Move all 900 lines into each developer's user-level CLAUDE.md to reduce project repository noise.
choices.explanation: Shared project standards belong in project-controlled memory. Moving them to user memory creates drift and hides team requirements from version control.

choices.text: Convert every section into a slash command so no instructions are loaded until a developer chooses one.
choices.explanation: Slash commands are useful for explicit workflows, but always-applicable standards should remain available through memory and rules.

knowledge: Explicit criteria work better than vague conservatism.

guideReferences: Domain 4, Task 4.1: Design prompts with explicit criteria to improve precision and reduce false positives
questions.guideSummary: Covers disabling noisy categories while improving prompt criteria for actionable review output.
questions.prompt: A dependency review assistant flags every package version that is not the newest release. Most findings are unactionable because the team's policy only requires action for known vulnerabilities, deprecated packages, or unsupported major versions. What is the best prompt-level correction?

choices.text: Ask Claude to include fewer findings by returning only the top five dependency concerns.
choices.explanation: A count cap can hide important findings and still include noisy ones. It does not define what should be reported.

choices.text: Keep the prompt unchanged but require each finding to include a package name and installed version.
choices.explanation: Structured fields improve readability, but the review will still flag the wrong category of issue.

choices.text: Upgrade to a larger model so it can better infer which package updates matter.
choices.explanation: Model capability is not the main gap. The policy boundary needs to be stated explicitly.

choices.text: Disable the 'latest version available' category and define reportable dependency issues with concrete severity examples.
choices.explanation: Correct. The noisy category should be removed while the real risk categories get explicit criteria.

skills: Add escalation criteria with examples.
Honor explicit human-agent requests immediately.
Acknowledge frustration while resolving straightforward cases.
Ask for identifiers rather than guessing among multiple matches.
knowledge: Escalation triggers include human requests, policy gaps, and inability to progress.
Straightforward issues may be resolved even when a customer is frustrated unless they request a human.
Sentiment and self-reported confidence are weak proxies for complexity.
Multiple matches require clarification.
guideReferences: Domain 5, Task 5.2: Design effective escalation and ambiguity resolution patterns
questions.guideSummary: Escalate when policy coverage is missing or progress is blocked, not merely because sentiment is negative.
questions.prompt: A subscription-support agent receives a request to waive a nonrefundable cancellation fee because the customer was unexpectedly deployed overseas. The published policy covers medical emergencies and duplicate charges, but not military deployment. The customer is calm and has not asked for a human. What should the agent do?

choices.text: Deny the waiver because the exact exception is not listed in policy.
choices.explanation: A policy gap is not the same as a clear denial. The agent should not invent a final policy decision where guidance is missing.

choices.text: Escalate with a structured handoff describing the request, missing policy coverage, account state, and recommended review question.
choices.explanation: Correct. Policy gaps are escalation triggers even when the customer is polite and the case seems straightforward.

choices.text: Use sentiment as the deciding signal and continue handling the case automatically because the customer is calm.
choices.explanation: Sentiment is a weak proxy for complexity. Calm customers can still need human judgment for uncovered policy cases.

choices.text: Approve the waiver because the deployment sounds similar to a medical emergency exception.
choices.explanation: Guessing by analogy creates an unauthorized policy exception and bypasses the needed review.

skills: Select subagents dynamically based on the problem.
Partition scope to reduce duplicated or missing coverage.
Route subagent communication through the coordinator for observability and recovery.
knowledge: Hub-and-spoke coordinator architecture.
Subagents do not automatically inherit coordinator context.
Coordinator responsibilities for decomposition, delegation, aggregation, and gap handling.
guideReferences: Domain 1, Task 1.2: Orchestrate multi-agent systems with coordinator-subagent patterns
questions.guideSummary: A coordinator should partition work, delegate to isolated specialists, aggregate outputs, and handle gaps rather than letting subagents duplicate or miss coverage.
questions.prompt: A security audit system has dependency, infrastructure, and application-code subagents. Production reviews show duplicated findings in dependency and application-code reports, while cloud storage policy findings are often missed. Each subagent receives the entire audit request and decides independently what to inspect. What orchestration change best addresses the issue?

choices.text: Have the coordinator assign explicit scopes to each specialist, collect their outputs, and request targeted follow-up for uncovered areas.
choices.explanation: Correct. The coordinator is responsible for decomposition, routing, aggregation, and gap handling in a hub-and-spoke design.

choices.text: Let subagents communicate directly with each other so they can negotiate who owns each finding.
choices.explanation: Direct sibling coordination reduces coordinator observability and makes recovery harder. Communication should route through the coordinator.

choices.text: Run the same prompt through all subagents and deduplicate duplicate findings after synthesis.
choices.explanation: Post-hoc deduplication does not fix missing coverage and wastes work by preserving the poor partitioning.

choices.text: Replace the specialist agents with one general security agent that has every available tool.
choices.explanation: A single broad agent loses the isolation and specialization benefits and can make tool selection less reliable.

skills: Return errorCategory, isRetryable, and readable descriptions.
Mark business-rule errors as non-retryable with customer-safe explanations.
Recover locally from transient failures where possible.
Distinguish access failure from valid empty results.
knowledge: MCP isError flag pattern.
Transient, validation, business, and permission error categories.
Generic failures prevent useful recovery.
Retryability must be explicit.
guideReferences: Domain 2, Task 2.2: Implement structured error responses for MCP tools
questions.guideSummary: MCP tools should return `isError` with structured categories, retryability, and readable context so agents can recover appropriately.
questions.prompt: A `create_shipping_quote` MCP tool rejects requests with invalid postal codes, times out during carrier outages, and denies quotes for embargoed destinations. Today every failure returns `{ "isError": true, "content": [{ "type": "text", "text": "Quote failed" }] }`. Claude either retries too much or asks users for information they already gave. What response design best supports reliable recovery?

choices.text: Return a successful empty quote object for all failures and let Claude infer what happened from the missing fields.
choices.explanation: Empty success responses blur real errors with valid empty results and remove recovery information.

choices.text: Include `errorCategory`, `isRetryable`, a customer-safe message, and any validation field details in the MCP error response.
choices.explanation: Correct. Validation, transient, and business-rule errors need different recovery paths, so the tool should expose those distinctions directly.

choices.text: Throw exceptions from the MCP handler so Claude Code logs the stack trace for investigation.
choices.explanation: A stack trace helps operators but does not give Claude a structured tool result it can use to recover.

choices.text: Return the carrier's raw error string exactly as received to preserve detail.
choices.explanation: Raw messages may contain useful clues, but without category and retryability Claude still has to guess.

guideReferences: Domain 3, Task 3.2: Create and configure custom slash commands and skills
questions.guideSummary: Covers choosing repository-scoped slash commands for shared, repeatable team workflows.
questions.prompt: A team repeatedly asks Claude Code to run the same pre-release workflow: inspect the changelog, compare changed packages, update release notes, and produce a final checklist. They want the workflow available to every contributor and reviewed with normal pull requests. Where should they put it?

choices.text: In each contributor's ~/.claude/commands/ directory so everyone can customize the release process locally.
choices.explanation: User commands are personal. They are not the right place for a team workflow that must stay consistent and reviewable.

choices.text: In the root CLAUDE.md so the release workflow is loaded during every Claude Code interaction.
choices.explanation: A release workflow is on-demand. Loading detailed procedural steps for every task wastes context and can distract unrelated work.

choices.text: Skip a saved command and ask Claude Code at each release to infer the steps from how recent release pull requests were prepared.
choices.explanation: Reconstructing the workflow from past pull requests each time is non-deterministic and drifts as the history grows, so contributors get different steps. A version-controlled project command captures the exact reviewable workflow once.

choices.text: In a project slash command under .claude/commands/ so the workflow is version-controlled with the repository.
choices.explanation: Correct. Repository-scoped commands are designed for shared team workflows that should live in version control.

skills: Create 2-4 targeted examples.
Show the desired output format.
Distinguish acceptable patterns from real issues.
Demonstrate extraction across varied document formats.
knowledge: Few-shot examples are strong for consistent actionable output.
Examples teach ambiguous-case handling.
Examples help generalize judgment rather than match fixed cases.
Examples reduce hallucination in extraction tasks.
guideReferences: Domain 4, Task 4.2: Apply few-shot prompting to improve output consistency and quality
questions.guideSummary: Covers using few-shot examples to teach ambiguous-case handling and improve consistent judgment.
questions.prompt: A support agent should escalate policy exceptions but handle ordinary refund requests. In testing, it escalates angry but eligible customers and handles calm customers whose cases require manager approval. Which change best teaches the intended escalation judgment?

choices.text: Tell the agent to escalate only when it feels uncertain about the customer's intent.
choices.explanation: Uncertainty about intent is not the policy boundary and can reproduce the same inconsistent behavior.

choices.text: Add 2-4 few-shot examples covering eligible refunds, explicit policy exceptions, angry-but-routine cases, and calm-but-unauthorized cases.
choices.explanation: Correct. Targeted examples teach the ambiguous boundary and prevent sentiment from becoming the escalation rule.

choices.text: Require escalation whenever the customer's message contains negative sentiment.
choices.explanation: That encodes the current false-positive pattern instead of teaching policy authorization.

choices.text: Remove escalation examples so the model generalizes from the written refund policy alone.
choices.explanation: The system is already failing to apply the policy consistently. Examples are useful for this kind of judgment boundary.

skills: Return failure type, attempted query, partial results, and alternatives.
Distinguish empty successful results from failures.
Recover locally before escalating.
Annotate synthesis coverage gaps.
knowledge: Structured error context enables recovery decisions.
Access failures differ from valid empty results.
Generic errors hide useful recovery context.
Suppressing errors and terminating on single failures are both anti-patterns.
guideReferences: Domain 5, Task 5.3: Implement error propagation strategies across multi-agent systems
questions.guideSummary: Propagate structured failure context and partial results so a coordinator can recover without hiding gaps.
questions.prompt: In a multi-agent portfolio review, the tax-lot subagent retrieves holdings for eight accounts but gets a permission error on the ninth. The coordinator must produce a risk summary for an advisor meeting in 10 minutes. What should the subagent return?

choices.text: Return a structured failure type, the attempted account identifier, successful partial results, and possible recovery alternatives.
choices.explanation: Correct. The coordinator needs both partial value and explicit failure context to annotate coverage or choose a recovery path.

choices.text: Return only the eight successful accounts so the coordinator can complete the report without distraction.
choices.explanation: Suppressing the access failure makes the final summary look complete when one account is missing.

choices.text: Return an empty result for the ninth account because no tax lots were available.
choices.explanation: A permission error is not a valid empty result. Treating it as empty hides access and coverage risk.

choices.text: Terminate the entire workflow because any subagent failure could make the final report unreliable.
choices.explanation: Stopping on a single failure overreacts when useful partial results and a clear coverage gap can still be reported.

questions.guideSummary: The coordinator should dynamically select subagents based on task requirements instead of forcing every request through a fixed pipeline.
questions.prompt: An employee onboarding assistant always invokes benefits, device-provisioning, facilities, and payroll subagents. Simple questions like "When does dental coverage start?" take 30 seconds because all four subagents run. Complex onboarding cases still benefit from multiple specialists. What architecture is most appropriate?

choices.text: Make the coordinator classify each request and invoke only the subagents needed for that request, then aggregate their results.
choices.explanation: Correct. Dynamic selection lets the coordinator adapt subagent fan-out to the problem instead of paying full pipeline cost every time.

choices.text: Keep the fixed pipeline but reduce each subagent prompt to a shorter version for simple requests.
choices.explanation: Shorter prompts reduce some cost but still route every request through unnecessary specialists.

choices.text: Send simple questions directly to the payroll subagent because it has the most complete employee record.
choices.explanation: Hardwiring one specialist for broad simple questions creates misrouting when the topic is benefits, devices, or facilities.

choices.text: Ask the user to choose which specialist should handle each onboarding question.
choices.explanation: That pushes orchestration work to the user. The coordinator should route based on the request.

skills: Return errorCategory, isRetryable, and readable descriptions.
Mark business-rule errors as non-retryable with customer-safe explanations.
Recover locally from transient failures where possible.
Distinguish access failure from valid empty results.
knowledge: MCP isError flag pattern.
Transient, validation, business, and permission error categories.
Generic failures prevent useful recovery.

guideReferences: Domain 2, Task 2.2: Implement structured error responses for MCP tools
questions.guideSummary: Agents need to distinguish valid empty results from permission failures because those outcomes require different user-facing actions.
questions.prompt: A directory MCP tool `find_employee_records` currently returns `[]` when no employees match and also returns `[]` when the caller lacks access to the department. Claude tells managers that no records exist when the real issue is missing authorization. Which change best aligns with MCP error handling guidance?

choices.text: Return an empty list for both cases but add an audit log entry when access is denied.
choices.explanation: The audit log may help compliance, but Claude still receives the wrong semantic signal.

choices.text: Always ask the user to confirm the department before calling `find_employee_records`.
choices.explanation: User confirmation does not solve authorization ambiguity in the tool result.

choices.text: Retry the directory lookup with a broader department scope when the result is empty.
choices.explanation: Broader retries can worsen permission issues and still treat access failures as search misses.

choices.text: Return a successful empty list only for valid no-match searches, and return `isError: true` with `errorCategory: permission` for access failures.
choices.explanation: Correct. Empty results and permission errors are different states and should be represented differently to the agent.

questions.prompt: Security engineers want a reusable Claude Code workflow for exploratory dependency threat modeling. The workflow can be verbose, should not pollute the main implementation context, needs a required package name argument, and should only use read/search tools. What is the strongest design?

choices.text: Create a skill with frontmatter that uses context: fork, an argument-hint for the package name, and allowed-tools limited to read/search tools.
choices.explanation: Correct. Skills support on-demand workflows, optional context isolation, argument hints, and tool restrictions.

choices.text: Add the workflow to the root CLAUDE.md and instruct Claude Code to remember not to edit files during security reviews.
choices.explanation: This loads verbose review guidance all the time and relies on a prompt instead of tool restrictions.

choices.text: Create a user-level slash command for each security engineer and document the required package name in team chat.
choices.explanation: User commands are not shared through the repo, and documenting arguments outside the workflow is easy to miss.

choices.text: Run the threat-modeling steps in the normal implementation session and prompt Claude Code to stay read-only and not let the verbose output crowd out the current work.
choices.explanation: Running it inline relies on a prompt for the read-only and isolation guarantees the scenario needs; context: fork isolates the verbose output and allowed-tools enforces read-only deterministically.

skills: Create 2-4 targeted examples.
Show the desired output format.
Distinguish acceptable patterns from real issues.

questions.guideSummary: Covers few-shot examples that demonstrate extraction behavior across varied document formats.
questions.prompt: Your extractor reads maintenance reports and should output normalized equipment failures. It works for reports with a 'Failure' heading but misses failures described in technician notes or bullet lists. The schema is correct and the missed facts are present in the source. What is the most effective next improvement?

choices.text: Make the failure field non-nullable so validation rejects records where no failure is extracted.
choices.explanation: That catches the miss after the fact, but it does not teach the model how to find failures in varied formats.

choices.text: Retry every report three times and keep the extraction with the most failure records.
choices.explanation: Voting by count can reward over-extraction and still does not provide the missing pattern guidance.

choices.text: Post-process the output by filling missing failures with 'unknown'.
choices.explanation: Filling with 'unknown' discards source facts that are actually present and needed downstream.

choices.text: Add few-shot examples showing complete extractions from reports with headings, notes, and bullet-list formats.
choices.explanation: Correct. The model needs examples that demonstrate where the same field appears across varied document structures.

skills: Spawn focused exploration subagents.

questions.guideSummary: Use scratchpads, focused exploration, summaries, and recovery state to keep large codebase investigations reliable.
questions.prompt: A code assistant is planning a migration across a 900-package monorepo. After hours of searches, its recommendations become generic and it forgets which packages already had custom adapters. What exploration pattern best addresses the problem?

choices.text: Keep all search output in the conversation so the model can revisit any line if needed.
choices.explanation: Raw search output can fill context quickly and worsen attention dilution across a large investigation.

choices.text: Spawn focused exploration subagents, maintain a scratchpad of key findings and package status, summarize between phases, and compact when discovery output grows too large.
choices.explanation: Correct. Focused exploration plus persistent state keeps findings available without carrying every verbose transcript forward.

choices.text: Restart with a new session whenever the assistant starts giving generic answers.
choices.explanation: A fresh session can reduce drift, but without a recovery manifest or scratchpad it loses hard-won codebase knowledge.

choices.text: Ask the model to produce a final migration plan after the first broad search pass.
choices.explanation: A single broad pass is unlikely to capture package-specific exceptions or cross-file dependencies reliably.

questions.prompt: A claims review coordinator is instructed to delegate medical coding, policy coverage, and fraud checks to specialist subagents. In production, it keeps explaining which specialists should be consulted but never actually starts them. The agent definition for the coordinator has no `Task` tool in its allowed tools. What is the best fix?

choices.text: Add stronger wording telling the coordinator that delegation is mandatory before it answers.
choices.explanation: The coordinator cannot spawn subagents without the required tool, regardless of prompt wording.

choices.text: Allow the coordinator to use `Task` and define the specialist agents with descriptions, prompts, and appropriate tool sets.
choices.explanation: Correct. Subagent spawning requires the Task tool, and the subagents need usable definitions for the coordinator to invoke.

choices.text: Give the coordinator every specialist tool so it can perform all checks in one conversation.
choices.explanation: That removes the intended specialist isolation and does not configure subagent invocation.

choices.text: Have each specialist poll a shared queue for new work instead of being invoked by the coordinator.
choices.explanation: A polling queue is extra infrastructure and bypasses the Task-based spawning pattern described in the guide.

questions.guideSummary: Agents should receive only the tools needed for their role, with constrained alternatives replacing broad generic tools when possible.
questions.prompt: A support platform gives every subagent the same 28 tools, including refund processing, account suspension, invoice lookup, shipment tracing, and internal notes editing. The shipment subagent occasionally issues refunds because refund-related tool names appear in context during delayed-package conversations. What architecture change best reduces these errors?

choices.text: Keep all tools available but add a warning to the shipment subagent prompt saying refunds are dangerous.
choices.explanation: Prompt warnings are weaker than removing irrelevant high-impact tools from the agent's available set.

choices.text: Rename `process_refund` to `dangerous_process_refund` so Claude avoids it unless necessary.
choices.explanation: Renaming alone does not enforce role boundaries and can still leave an irrelevant tool available.

choices.text: Move all support work back into one generalist agent so there are no subagent tool mismatches.
choices.explanation: A generalist with every tool has the same or greater selection burden and loses the benefit of specialization.

choices.text: Restrict each subagent to role-appropriate tools and give the shipment subagent only shipment tools plus narrowly scoped read-only order context.
choices.explanation: Correct. Scoped tool access improves selection reliability and prevents specialized agents from acting outside their role.

questions.prompt: A design system convention applies to every React component file ending in .tsx, whether it lives in apps/, sites/, or namespaces/. A root CLAUDE.md currently describes the convention globally, and Claude Code sometimes applies it while editing non-React files. What is the best adjustment?

choices.text: Remove the convention from memory and instead tell Claude Code to match the style of sibling .tsx components in whatever directory it is editing.
choices.explanation: Inferring from sibling files reproduces whatever inconsistencies already exist and gives no stable standard. A path-scoped rule states the convention explicitly and still loads only for .tsx files.

choices.text: Create a .claude/rules file with paths globs that match the relevant .tsx files.
choices.explanation: Correct. Path-specific rules load the convention only when matching files are being edited, regardless of directory.

choices.text: Move the convention to user-level CLAUDE.md so only frontend engineers load it.
choices.explanation: The convention is shared project behavior. User memory would not reliably apply for every contributor.

choices.text: Turn the convention into a slash command and ask engineers to run it after editing components.
choices.explanation: This makes a file convention manual. Path-scoped rules are better for conditional convention loading.

questions.guideSummary: Covers using a forced tool choice when a specific schema-backed extraction must happen before other work.
questions.prompt: A research pipeline exposes `extract_claims`, `search_web`, and `write_summary`. User requests often say, 'Find claims in this report and verify them.' In testing, Claude sometimes calls `search_web` first, before extracting claim text and source locations. How should the first API call be configured?

choices.text: Force `extract_claims` with `tool_choice`, then allow search tools in later turns after the structured claims are available.
choices.explanation: Correct. A forced specific tool call guarantees the first step produces the schema-backed claims needed for verification.

choices.text: Set `tool_choice` to `auto` and list `extract_claims` before the other tools.
choices.explanation: Tool order is not a reliable contract for sequencing. Auto still allows the wrong first tool.

choices.text: Set `tool_choice` to `any` so Claude must use one of the available tools.
choices.explanation: `any` guarantees a tool call, not the specific extraction tool needed first.

choices.text: Remove `search_web` until after extraction is complete and run all verification manually.
choices.explanation: Changing the available tools can work, but forced tool choice is the direct API-level way to guarantee the required first call.

skills: Sample high-confidence outputs across segments.
Analyze accuracy by document type and field.
Calibrate confidence thresholds with labeled sets.
Route low confidence or ambiguous records to human review.
knowledge: Aggregate accuracy can hide weak segments.
Stratified sampling detects errors in high-confidence outputs.
Field-level confidence needs labeled validation calibration.
Accuracy should be checked by document type and field.
guideReferences: Domain 5, Task 5.5: Design human review workflows and confidence calibration
questions.guideSummary: Calibrate confidence thresholds with labeled data and analyze accuracy by segment before reducing human review.
questions.prompt: An insurance extraction system reports 96% accuracy for records with confidence above 0.92, and the team wants to auto-approve those records. The labeled set includes auto, home, and specialty claims, but the aggregate metric is not broken down. What is the most important next validation step?

choices.text: Auto-approve a small percentage of high-confidence records and wait for downstream complaints.
choices.explanation: A pilot can be useful later, but discovering segment failures through customer harm is the wrong first validation step.

choices.text: Analyze high-confidence accuracy by claim type and extracted field, using labeled examples before changing routing.
choices.explanation: Correct. Aggregate accuracy can hide weak segments, especially for specific document types or fields.

choices.text: Raise the threshold from 0.92 to 0.98 so only the most confident records bypass review.
choices.explanation: A higher threshold is not calibrated if confidence is misaligned for a particular segment.

choices.text: Trust the model confidence because the aggregate accuracy already exceeds the operational target.
choices.explanation: Self-reported confidence needs validation against labeled data; an aggregate target alone is not enough.

questions.prompt: A sales-intelligence coordinator asks a competitor-pricing subagent to "compare these results with the account plan." The subagent replies that no account plan was provided. The coordinator had read the account plan earlier in its own session. What should the coordinator do differently?

choices.text: Use the same model and API key for both agents so the subagent can see the coordinator's history.
choices.explanation: Shared credentials do not create shared conversation context between agent invocations.

choices.text: Ask the pricing subagent to infer the account plan from the competitor-pricing data.
choices.explanation: Inferring missing context risks fabrication and does not preserve source-backed facts.

choices.text: Include the relevant account-plan facts and source metadata directly in the Task prompt for the pricing subagent.
choices.explanation: Correct. Subagent contexts are isolated; required prior findings must be explicitly passed into the invocation.

choices.text: Store the account plan in the coordinator's system prompt for the rest of the session.
choices.explanation: That still does not automatically transfer the facts to an isolated subagent invocation.

skills: Restrict each subagent to role-appropriate tools.
Replace generic tools with constrained alternatives.

knowledge: Too many tools degrade model selection reliability.

tool_choice supports auto, any, and forced tool selection.
guideReferences: Domain 2, Task 2.3: Distribute tools appropriately across agents and configure tool choice
questions.guideSummary: `tool_choice` should be forced when a specific tool call is a required first step, then relaxed when later steps need model choice.
questions.prompt: A refund workflow must always call `check_refund_policy` before any refund is created. The same agent also has `lookup_order`, `message_customer`, and `process_refund`. In testing, Claude sometimes answers from memory of the policy or calls `process_refund` first. What is the best use of `tool_choice`?

choices.text: Use `tool_choice: auto` and add stronger instructions that policy checks are mandatory.
choices.explanation: `auto` permits no tool call or the wrong first call, so it does not enforce the required prerequisite.

choices.text: Use `tool_choice: any` for every turn so Claude must call some tool before answering.
choices.explanation: `any` forces a tool call but not the required policy tool, and using it every turn can force unnecessary calls.

choices.text: Force `process_refund` first and have that tool internally check the policy.
choices.explanation: That hides the explicit workflow requirement and risks combining eligibility checks with side effects.

choices.text: Force `check_refund_policy` for the first step, then return to `auto` after its result is in context.
choices.explanation: Correct. A forced tool choice guarantees the prerequisite call, while `auto` later allows lookup, messaging, or refund processing as appropriate.

skills: Create rule files with path globs.
Apply conventions by file type regardless of directory.

questions.guideSummary: Covers applying conditional instructions by file path and extension instead of loading all conventions globally.
questions.prompt: Database migration files appear under several packages: services/billing/migrations, apps/admin/db/migrations, and namespaces/work-kit/*/migrations. They all require reversible operations and explicit rollback notes. The rule is currently in root CLAUDE.md and distracts Claude Code during ordinary TypeScript edits. What should the team do?

choices.text: Move the migration guidance into a .claude/rules file with paths patterns that match migration files across the repo.
choices.explanation: Correct. A path-scoped rule loads the database convention only for matching migration paths, even when those paths span directories.

choices.text: Leave the rule in root CLAUDE.md because migration mistakes are high impact and should always be visible.
choices.explanation: High impact does not mean always relevant. Loading unrelated guidance can reduce focus for other tasks.

choices.text: Place a CLAUDE.md file in only the largest migrations directory and make it the canonical source.
choices.explanation: That would miss migration files in other directories unless the guidance is duplicated or imported appropriately.

choices.text: Drop the memory guidance and add a CI check that fails the build when a migration lacks a rollback note or reversible operations.
choices.explanation: A CI gate catches violations only after the migration is written and forces rework. A path-scoped rule loads the reversibility convention while Claude Code is editing migration files, preventing the violation up front.

skills: Define extraction tools with JSON schemas.

Make fields optional or nullable when sources may omit them.

questions.guideSummary: Covers designing schemas with nullable fields and an other-plus-detail enum pattern for real-world source variation.
questions.prompt: A claims extractor uses an enum for `incident_type`: `theft`, `fire`, `water`, and `liability`. Production documents include rare categories such as cyber incident and equipment breakdown. When those appear, the model either fails validation or maps them to the closest wrong enum. What schema change best preserves quality?

choices.text: Remove the enum and make `incident_type` a free-form string for every record.
choices.explanation: That avoids validation failures but loses the useful normalization guarantee for common cases.

choices.text: Keep the enum fixed and add examples mapping rare categories to the nearest common category.
choices.explanation: Forcing cyber incidents into an unrelated category creates semantic errors that pass validation.

choices.text: Add an `other` enum value plus an `incident_type_detail` field, and instruct the model to use it when no listed category fits.
choices.explanation: Correct. This keeps common categories normalized while preserving uncommon details without forcing lossy mappings.

choices.text: Make `incident_type` required and non-nullable so Claude cannot omit uncertain values.
choices.explanation: Required fields can increase fabrication when the source value does not fit the allowed categories.

skills: Require claim-source mappings from subagents.
Separate established findings from contested ones.
Pass conflicting values to the coordinator with attribution.
Require publication dates for temporal interpretation.
Render different content types in appropriate formats.

Structured claim-source mappings must survive synthesis.
Conflicting credible statistics should be annotated, not arbitrarily chosen.
Temporal data needs publication or collection dates.

questions.guideSummary: Preserve claim-source mappings, temporal metadata, and uncertainty labels when credible sources conflict.
questions.prompt: A regulatory reporting assistant combines three credible sources about a compliance deadline. One agency FAQ says the deadline is June 30, a later bulletin says July 15 for small entities, and a trade association summary omits the exception date. What should the synthesis layer do?

choices.text: Choose the most common date among sources and present it as the deadline.
choices.explanation: Counting sources can discard a later or more specific authority and hide the small-entity exception.

choices.text: Drop the trade association source and summarize only the agency sources without citations.
choices.explanation: Removing a weaker source may be reasonable, but losing citations and dates still undermines provenance.

choices.text: Present both credible deadline claims with source attribution, publication dates, applicability conditions, and an uncertainty note where the sources differ.
choices.explanation: Correct. Conflicting credible statistics or dates should be carried forward with attribution and temporal context.

choices.text: Ask the final writer to infer the correct date from the prose summary.
choices.explanation: Inference after flattening evidence is exactly where provenance and uncertainty are lost.

questions.guideSummary: Independent subagent work can be spawned in parallel with multiple Task calls in one response when scopes and inputs are explicit.
questions.prompt: A legal brief cites eight independent exhibits. The coordinator currently asks one evidence subagent to review them sequentially, causing long latency. The exhibits do not depend on each other, and the coordinator needs a consolidated issue list at the end. Which invocation pattern best reduces latency while preserving coordination?

choices.text: Give the evidence subagent a larger context window so it can process all exhibits in one pass.
choices.explanation: A larger context does not address sequential latency when the units are independent.

choices.text: Ask the evidence subagent to summarize every exhibit first, then run a second full review over the summaries.
choices.explanation: This adds another pass and may lose details before review. It does not exploit parallel independent work.

choices.text: Let each evidence subagent send conclusions directly to the final report generator.
choices.explanation: Bypassing the coordinator weakens aggregation, observability, and gap handling.

choices.text: Spawn separate evidence-review subagents with multiple `Task` calls, each receiving one exhibit and a shared output schema.
choices.explanation: Correct. Independent work can be parallelized through multiple Task calls while the coordinator remains responsible for aggregation.

questions.prompt: A team wants Claude Code to use the same internal schema-inspection MCP server in a repository. One developer added it only to their user-level Claude config with a hardcoded token, so teammates do not see the server and the token cannot rotate cleanly. What configuration approach is best?

choices.text: Commit the token directly into a project `.mcp.json` so everyone has the same working server.
choices.explanation: Project scope is appropriate for the shared server, but committing secrets is not.

choices.text: Put the shared server definition in project `.mcp.json` and reference credentials through environment variable expansion.
choices.explanation: Correct. Project scope shares the integration, and environment expansion keeps credentials out of durable repository config.

choices.text: Ask every developer to copy the original user-level config into `~/.claude.json`.
choices.explanation: Manual per-user copying makes the shared server drift and repeats the credential-management problem.

choices.text: Expose the server only through Bash commands so no MCP configuration is needed.
choices.explanation: This gives up MCP discovery, tool descriptions, and structured resources for a shared integration.

Explore subagents can isolate verbose discovery.

questions.prompt: A product team asks Claude Code to redesign authorization checks across six services. The request involves finding current patterns, identifying ownership boundaries, proposing a migration path, and then making code changes. Which workflow should the engineer choose?

choices.text: Use direct execution immediately so Claude Code can discover requirements while editing files.
choices.explanation: Direct execution fits clear, narrow changes. Here the architecture and migration path are not yet understood.

choices.text: Create a slash command that always performs authorization rewrites without prior discussion.
choices.explanation: A fixed command is too rigid for a broad redesign with uncertain service-specific tradeoffs.

choices.text: Start in plan mode to explore and agree on an approach, then switch to direct implementation once the scope is clear.
choices.explanation: Correct. Broad, uncertain, multi-file architecture work benefits from planning before edits.

choices.text: Put the redesign request into CLAUDE.md so Claude Code applies it to future authorization edits.
choices.explanation: This is a project task, not a durable instruction that should affect future unrelated work.

Identify retryable versus non-retryable failures.
Capture detected_pattern for feedback analysis.
Use self-correction fields such as calculated totals and conflict flags.

Semantic validation differs from schema syntax validation.

questions.prompt: Your extraction job validates medication records. A failed record has `dose_mg` as the string `5mg` instead of a number and `frequency_per_day` as `twice` instead of an integer. The source text clearly contains the needed values. What should the retry request include?

choices.text: The original document, the failed extraction, and specific validation errors explaining the expected numeric fields.
choices.explanation: Correct. Specific feedback plus the source and failed output gives the model enough context to correct retryable formatting errors.

choices.text: Only the validation schema, so the model is not biased by the previous failed extraction.
choices.explanation: The model still needs the original source values and benefits from seeing exactly what failed.

choices.text: Only a generic instruction saying the previous answer was invalid and should be fixed.
choices.explanation: Generic feedback is weaker than concrete field-level validation errors.

choices.text: A request to infer the missing numeric values from typical medication dosing guidelines.
choices.explanation: The values should come from the source, not domain guesses. The source already contains the needed data.

questions.prompt: An incident-response assistant must hand off a postmortem draft after ingesting logs, chat excerpts, deploy records, and status-page updates. The context window cannot hold all raw material, and prior summaries have shifted the outage start time by several minutes. What context should be passed to the final writer?

choices.text: A narrative summary of the incident plus a note that raw logs are available if needed.
choices.explanation: A prose-only summary is the failure mode already observed; exact times and source bindings can drift.

choices.text: The full raw logs and chat transcript, with no summary, so the final writer can decide what matters.
choices.explanation: Full raw context is inefficient and can bury the exact facts the final writer needs.

choices.text: A structured timeline with exact timestamps, claim-source links, unresolved uncertainties, and a concise narrative summary.
choices.explanation: Correct. The timeline preserves critical facts and provenance while the summary supplies readable context.

choices.text: Only the status-page updates because they are customer-facing and therefore authoritative.
choices.explanation: Customer-facing updates may omit internal causes or have delayed timestamps; they should be mapped, not used alone.

skills: Block downstream tools until prerequisites are satisfied.
Decompose multi-concern requests before synthesizing a response.

Critical business rules need deterministic compliance.
Handoffs should include root cause, customer or case state, and recommended actions.

questions.guideSummary: Critical workflow prerequisites should be enforced programmatically by blocking downstream tools until required checks are complete.
questions.prompt: A loan assistant must not call `approve_loan` until identity verification and income checks have both passed. Prompt instructions say this clearly, but logs show rare approvals after only one check. What design provides the required enforcement?

choices.text: Put a deterministic gate in the workflow layer that blocks `approve_loan` unless both prerequisite results are present and passing.
choices.explanation: Correct. Critical business order must be enforced programmatically, not left to model discretion.

choices.text: Add more examples to the system prompt showing the correct order for common loan cases.
choices.explanation: Examples can improve behavior but cannot guarantee a required workflow order.

choices.text: Ask the model to produce a checklist before every approval and trust the checklist result.
choices.explanation: A model-generated checklist is still discretionary and can be wrong or skipped.

choices.text: Run identity verification and income checks in parallel after approval to reduce latency.
choices.explanation: That violates the prerequisite requirement; the checks must pass before the downstream action.

questions.prompt: A folder named core-utils was renamed to platform-utils last week. CI fails on one package because its test script still points at the old path, and the engineer hands Claude Code the exact script line and the exact replacement string. But the old name core-utils is a generic token that also appears in tsconfig path aliases, a few other packages' build scripts, and many import statements across the monorepo, and nobody has confirmed which of those were already updated. How should Claude Code approach this?

choices.text: Use direct execution to apply the exact replacement string to the one failing script line and run that package's test.
choices.explanation: This clears the one failure the engineer noticed, but a rename of a generic token is rarely confined to a single line. Other stale core-utils references will fail later, so the scope is wider than the provided edit implies.

choices.text: Run a repo-wide search-and-replace of core-utils with platform-utils across all files to catch every occurrence at once.
choices.explanation: A blind global replace can corrupt unrelated matches such as comments, doc strings, or a similarly named unrelated symbol. The references need to be located and reviewed, not swapped indiscriminately.

choices.text: Spawn an Explore subagent to find every remaining core-utils reference across scripts, tsconfig aliases, and imports, then fix the failing line plus any other stale references it surfaces.
choices.explanation: Correct. The single CI failure is one visible symptom of a repo-wide rename. Context-heavy discovery with Explore finds the other stale references before they cause the next failure, which the bare replacement string would not.

choices.text: Create a repository slash command so future folder renames apply the same replacement automatically.
choices.explanation: A command formalizes a one-off cleanup into a shared workflow and still would not discover which references are currently stale. The immediate need is to find and fix the remaining references now.

Independent review instances catch issues the generator may miss.

questions.guideSummary: Covers splitting large reviews into local file passes and a separate cross-file integration pass.
questions.prompt: A PR review prompt sends a 22-file diff to one Claude call and asks for correctness, security, performance, and style feedback. Results are uneven: deep comments on the first files, shallow comments on later files, and missed API contract mismatches across files. What review architecture best addresses this?

choices.text: Run focused per-file or per-concern passes, then run a separate cross-file integration pass over structured findings and relevant interfaces.
choices.explanation: Correct. Multi-pass review reduces attention dilution and separates local issues from integration issues.

choices.text: Ask for shorter findings so the single review pass can cover all files more evenly.
choices.explanation: Shorter output does not fix attention dilution or cross-file reasoning gaps.

choices.text: Review only files with the largest diff because those are most likely to contain defects.
choices.explanation: Large files may matter, but cross-file contract bugs can live in small changes too.

choices.text: Use one pass but raise the maximum output tokens to allow more comments.
choices.explanation: More output room does not guarantee the model attended to every file or integration point.

guideReferences: Domain 1, Task 1.6: Design task decomposition strategies for complex workflows
questions.guideSummary: Open-ended investigations should use adaptive decomposition based on intermediate findings rather than a rigid sequence.
questions.prompt: An incident agent is asked why a checkout service intermittently times out. The cause could be network latency, a database lock, a recent deployment, or a third-party API. Early logs will determine the next best path. What decomposition approach is most appropriate?

choices.text: Execute a fixed pipeline that fully investigates network, database, deployment, and third-party systems in that order every time.
choices.explanation: A fixed pipeline wastes work and can delay the path indicated by early evidence.

choices.text: Ask four subagents to investigate all possible causes immediately and merge their reports.
choices.explanation: Fan-out can be useful after scoping, but immediate full parallelism is expensive when early evidence may narrow the search.

choices.text: Start with a lightweight map of likely components, inspect the strongest signal first, and adapt follow-up subtasks as evidence emerges.
choices.explanation: Correct. Open-ended debugging benefits from an adaptive plan driven by intermediate findings.

choices.text: Only inspect the most recent deployment because intermittent failures are usually deployment-related.
choices.explanation: This prematurely commits to one hypothesis instead of adapting to evidence.

questions.guideSummary: Resumed sessions can contain stale tool results; fresh sessions with explicit summaries may be safer when the underlying state changed materially.
questions.prompt: A code review session from yesterday found no issue in `BillingRules.ts`. Overnight, another engineer rewrote that file and its tests. The reviewer wants Claude to continue the old session and finish the approval. What is the best session-state choice?

choices.text: Use `--resume` without extra context so the model retains maximum continuity from yesterday.
choices.explanation: Blindly resuming preserves stale results and may cause the model to rely on outdated file contents.

choices.text: Resume the session and tell the model to trust its previous clean finding unless tests now fail.
choices.explanation: The changed file needs review even if tests pass; the old finding is no longer reliable.

choices.text: Start a fresh session with a concise summary of still-valid findings and explicitly tell it that `BillingRules.ts` changed and must be re-read.
choices.explanation: Correct. Material file changes make old tool results stale; a fresh summary avoids anchoring on invalid context.

choices.text: Fork the old session into two branches and approve if both branches reach the same conclusion.
choices.explanation: Forking stale context does not fix the underlying problem that both branches may rely on old tool results.

questions.prompt: After a discovery session maps a legacy reporting service, the team wants two independent Claude runs: one to explore a strangler migration and one to explore a full rewrite. Both should start from the same discovered baseline without contaminating each other's reasoning. What should you use?

choices.text: Resume the same named session twice and ask each run to ignore the other strategy.
choices.explanation: Reusing one session risks cross-contamination between strategies.

choices.text: Start two fresh sessions with no baseline so each strategy is unbiased.
choices.explanation: That loses the useful discovery context both branches need.

choices.text: Ask one agent to evaluate both strategies in a single response before any detailed investigation.
choices.explanation: A single response does not provide independent branches or deep strategy-specific exploration.

choices.text: Use `fork_session` from the discovery baseline and run one branch for each migration strategy.
choices.explanation: Correct. Forking creates independent branches from shared context for divergent exploration.

questions.guideSummary: Named session resumption is appropriate for valid ongoing investigations when the resumed agent is told what changed since the prior run.
questions.prompt: A regulatory evidence collection run was paused after cataloging 40 of 60 files. No source files changed, but two new files were added to the target folder. The team wants to continue without repeating the first 40 files. What is the best use of session state?

choices.text: Resume the named session and explicitly tell it which two files were added and that previously cataloged files remain unchanged.
choices.explanation: Correct. Resume is appropriate when prior work is still valid, and the changed state should be stated directly.

choices.text: Start over in a fresh session so the agent cannot be influenced by earlier catalog decisions.
choices.explanation: Starting over repeats valid work and is unnecessary when the prior state is current.

choices.text: Resume the session but avoid mentioning the new files so the agent discovers them naturally.
choices.explanation: The guide recommends telling resumed sessions exactly what changed to avoid stale assumptions.

choices.text: Fork the session before continuing so the original 40-file catalog is preserved as a safe checkpoint in case the rest of the run goes wrong.
choices.explanation: Forking is for exploring divergent paths, not as a backup mechanism; a straightforward resume continues the one valid investigation without a parallel branch to manage.
